final_text:
VRM Sales Library | To Sell Is Human | Daniel H. Pink
Post 1 of 3

OPERATORS WHO LISTEN MORE CLOSE MORE

Many VRM operators walk into a listing appointment with a prepared presentation and leave without an agreement. They covered everything: the technology stack, the review profile, the maintenance protocols, the fee structure. The owner said it was very helpful and they'd think about it.

That outcome is not a near-miss. It is the product of the wrong posture.

Daniel Pink's To Sell Is Human draws a distinction that most sales training skips: the difference between empathy and attunement. Empathy is feeling what another person feels. Attunement is temporarily stepping out of your own frame and into theirs, thinking like they think rather than feeling what they feel.

In a listing appointment, that distinction is most of the game.
notes: First post in series. Establishes the attunement posture and listening-first framework. Approved by editor.

final_text:
VRM Sales Library | To Sell Is Human | Daniel H. Pink
Post 2 of 3

THE OPERATORS SOLVING THE STATED PROBLEM ARE IN A PRICE COMPARISON

When an owner says 'my reviews have been dropping,' every manager they talk to promises better maintenance response times and higher cleaning standards. They are all solving the same stated problem. That is a feature comparison, and feature comparisons close on price.
notes: Strongest post in series. Problem-finding framework with three owner-complaint diagnostic scripts. Approved by editor.

final_text:
VRM Sales Library | To Sell Is Human | Daniel H. Pink
Post 3 of 3

THE LISTING APPOINTMENT IS A TRAP, UNLESS YOU REMOVE THE TRAP FEELING

Many VRM operators structure their close around preventing the no. They present everything, handle every objection, and push for a decision. The logic is that a well-defended presentation closes hesitant owners.

Pink's argument in To Sell Is Human is different. In a long-term relationship sale, the primary friction is not the price or the feature set. It is the perceived irreversibility of the decision.
notes: Off-ramp close and fit-disclosure question scripts. Approved by editor.
